questions.description: "Decisions of the members of a company may be taken either in general meeting or by written resolution or, informally,…
questions.text: "Decisions of the members of a company may be taken either in general meeting or by written resolution or, informally, by operation of the principle in Re Duomatic Limited [1969] 1 All ER 161". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "Corporate contractual capacity can be classified under the headings of substantive objects, express powers and implied…
questions.text: "Corporate contractual capacity can be classified under the headings of substantive objects, express powers and implied powers; everything else is ultra vires and void." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Richard and Frank are equal shareholders and directors in RF Events… Mortgage, Company.
questions.text: Richard and Frank are equal shareholders and directors in RF Events Limited (the "Company") which has been in the business of organising corporate events since 2005. Business was very strong for the last number of years and whilst the Company has a core staff of 12 employees, at times it employees, at times it employed up to 30 people. In the last year, business has never been worse. Clients are either not hosting events or cancelling events already planned. Richard and Frank have cut staff salaries by 20% and have stopped taking a salary for themselves. Even so, the Company is struggling to keep afloat and is behind on the rent for the premises and its bank loan of some €2,500,000 secured by a mortgage debenture, incorporating a floating charge and a fixed charge on the Company's premises, which is now worth only €1,750,000. This month it is unlikely to be able to pay to the revenue commissioners the full amount of VAT it collected. Richard and Frank believe that if the Company can only keep afloat for the next 6 months, it stands a good chance of surviving as they believe the worst of the recession is over. Frank has approached an old business colleague, now retired, and asked him to become an investor in the company offering him 25% of the Company (12 ½ % each from him and Richard). Matters came to a head yesterday when the Company's bank appointed a receiver on account of the Company's bank loan being in arrears. Richard and Frank approach you for advice on: (a) the steps involved in bringing a petition to have an examiner appointed to the Company; (b) the likelihood of success of a petition to have an examiner appointed to the Company; and (c) the consequence for the Company and its creditors of an examiner being appointed. You are required to advise Richard and Frank in relation to the matters set out at (a), (b) and (c) which should be clearly distinguished in your answer. You are required to refer to all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "Section 205 of the Companies Acts 1963 provides minority shareholders in an Irish company with a significant remedy…
questions.text: "Section 205 of the Companies Acts 1963 provides minority shareholders in an Irish company with a significant remedy where they are being oppressed or their interests are being disregarded. There are, however, a number of limitations on the availability of section 205 and on the remedies which it empowers the Court to grant." You are required to discuss the limitations to section 205 as described in the forgoing statement, citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Damien is the sole shareholder and one of two directors of DRK Limited… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Damien is the sole shareholder and one of two directors of DRK Limited (the "Company"), the other director of which is his wife, Deirdre. The Company operated the business of travel agent for a number of years. The Company has loan facilities from Big Bank Plc which are secured by a floating charge over the Company's assets, a fixed charge over its leasehold interest in the premises it rents and the personal guarantees of its directors, Damien and Deirdre. The six months ending 31 December 2009 were the worst ever experienced by the Company which made nearly all of its employees redundant. Wages were owed to some of the employees and one of them, Sean, was constantly calling to Damien's and Deirdre's family home seeking payment. In early January 2010, the revenue commissioners petitioned to have the company wound up on the grounds that it was unable to pay its debts and the High Court granted that petition, appointing Jack Harmer the official liquidator. You are retained by Jack to advise as to the law relating to the following payments by the Company: (a) Christmas week, when Sean called at the directors' home seeking payment, Damien was out and Deirdre, who was concerned at Sean's ever-threatening manner, wrote him a cheque for €5000 in respect of wages owed to him; (b) The morning that the petition was heard by the High Court, Damien transferred €30, 000 from one account the Company had into its overdrawn current account; (c) The day after the petition was presented, the Company's bank debited its current account for €10, 000 in respect of accrued interest on the Company's loans; (d) A month after the petition to wind up the Company, the liquidator himself made a payment from the Company's overdrawn account to the Company's landlord in respect of the premises it occupied as otherwise its lease would have been forfeited. You are required to advise Jack the liquidator on the law in relation to each of the four payments, distinguishing (a), (b), (c) and (d) in your answer. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: In November of 2009 James acquired all of the shares in a single-member… Company, Director.
questions.text: In November of 2009 James acquired all of the shares in a single-member private limited company called 'Dream On This Limited' (the "Company") which operated a retail business selling beds and mattresses. Prior to this the shares in the Company had been held by Jon who together with Aida were its only two directors. The deal to buy the shares in the company had been concluded quickly for €2,000,000 a sum which James considered to be at the lower end of the Company's value. Because he considered he was getting a good deal, James did not conduct a full due diligence of the Company and the sale was completed by stock transfer form alone without any share purchase agreement. Jon and Aida also resigned as directors of the Company and were replaced by James and his wife Jenny. In February of 2010 James approaches you for advice. He believes that he has discovered a number of irregularities in the conduct of the Company's affairs, drawing the following matters to your attention: (a) A contract which the Company had with the HSE for the supply of mattresses to the health service was assigned by the Company to a different company owned by Aida in August of 2009. In consequence, the Company's profitability has decreased significantly having lost one of its largest customers. (b) In March 2009 a loan of €150,000 was made by the Company to Jon which sum remains outstanding. On contacting Jon, James was told that it had been agreed with the Company at the time the loan was made that it would not be repaid for 5 years and that Jon was holding the Company to this arrangement. You are required to advise James on the liabilities of the former directors in respect of the matters detailed in paragraphs (a) and (b) which should be clearly distinguished in your answer. In your answer you must cote all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "Regulation 80 of Part I of Table A is the cornerstone of corporate governance in Irish companies." You are required to…
questions.text: "Regulation 80 of Part I of Table A is the cornerstone of corporate governance in Irish companies." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Last year Blue Chip Properties Limited (the "Company") were placed into… Mortgage, Company.
questions.text: Last year Blue Chip Properties Limited (the "Company") were placed into creditors' voluntary liquidation on the basis it was insolvent owing considerable sums to its bank, trade creditors and the revenue commissioners. The Company had acquired a portfolio of 20 apartments and town houses in Cork city which it let to its tenants. In the good times, after servicing the Company's borrowings, the rent flow from the properties produced a net revenue stream of about €100,000 per annum. The collapse in rental incomes, increased taxation, the collapse of property prices, and most recently flooding, brought the Company to its knees, and the decision was taken to place it into liquidation when it fell behind in VAT payments to the revenue commissioners, and payments to its trade creditors and bank. The Company's borrowings were provided by Big Bank plc (the "Bank") which had given it a series of term loans to acquire 20 properties. The Bank's security was a mortgage debenture comprising a floating charge, and also twenty fixed mortgages and charges over the 20 properties. The Bank also had a charge over an account into which the rent was paid in respect of the five most recently acquired investment properties, and the Company was unable to withdraw funds from that account without the consent of the Bank. The Bank has also appointed a receiver to the Company on foot of the mortgage debenture comprising the floating charge, and the receiver is collecting rents and using the proceeds to repay the Bank. Following his appointment, the Company's liquidator inspected the security which the Bank holds for its loans to the Company. The liquidator tells you that: (a) through an oversight, the mortgage debenture in favour of the Bank comprising the floating charge which contained the power to appoint the receiver was not registered in the Companies Registration Office under Section 99 of the Companies Act 1963; and (b) the charge over the account into which the rent was paid was registered in the Companies Registration Office as a fixed charge over the Company's book debts but the revenue commissioners are claiming priority of the amounts due to them ahead of sums due to the bank. You are required to advise the liquidator on the enforceability of the security described in (a) and (b) which should be clearly distinguished in your answer. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Denis was recently appointed liquidator of Southern Shopping Limited (the… Company, Director.
questions.text: Denis was recently appointed liquidator of Southern Shopping Limited (the "Company") which owns a chain of 4 supermarket-type shops in Munster. Denis was appointed liquidator to the Company after the directors placed it into creditors' voluntary liquidation on account of its insolvency. In the realisation of the Company's assets, Denis has received a number of solicitor's letters from the suppliers of goods to the Company. The letters demand return of goods on the grounds that the contracts under which the goods were supplied provided that title to the goods did not pass to the Company. Denis approaches you for advice in relation to the following cases: (a) Supplier A demands the return of all frozen beef, including that which had been cut-up into joints and steaks and minced into burgers by the Company. The retention of title in relation to the beef provided: "Title to the beef shall not pass to the Company until all sums due by the Company to Supplier A under any consignment of beef delivered to the Company have been paid in full". Supplier A claims that it is owed €380,000 from the Company for beef and that it is entitled to take that amount of beef from the Company's freezers. (b) Supplier B demands the return of all batteries which it supplied to the Company and also the proceeds of sale of any batteries sold for which the Company has not yet paid Supplier B. The retention of title clause in that case provided: "Ownership of the batteries supplied to the Company shall not pass until Supplier B has received in full the sums due to it from the Company and where the Company has sold batteries supplied by Supplier B, a trust in favour of Supplier B shall be impressed upon the proceeds of sale of those batteries". Supplier B is owed €120,000 by the Company and is claiming all of the batteries on the premises (worth €100,000) as well as another €20,000 from the Company's bank account. (c) Supplier C demands the return of garden furniture which it supplied to the Company. Its retention of title clause provided: "Beneficial ownership in the garden furniture supplied to the Company shall not pass to the Company until Supplier C has been paid the sums due to it by the Company". It is owed €230,000 by the Company and believes there is furniture worth this amount on the premises. None of the suppliers delivered particulars of the foregoing contractual provisions to the Companies Registration Office for registration. You are required to advise Denis as to whether any of the foregoing claims are valid or whether Denis can raise section 99 of the Companies Act 1963 on the grounds that they constitute unregistered charges. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "The authority to bind a company can arise through a director having ostensible authority and third parties, seeking to…
questions.text: "The authority to bind a company can arise through a director having ostensible authority and third parties, seeking to enforce contracts against companies entered into by directors, may also rely upon the Rule in Turquand's Case and SI 163 of 1973." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Hugh and Dermot are directors of and 50:50 shareholders in King Kanute… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Hugh and Dermot are directors of and 50:50 shareholders in King Kanute Kars Ltd (the "Company") which is in the business of buying and selling second hand cars. Business has been slow for the last 12 months and Hugh and Dermot have worked hard to sell cars cutting their margins significantly. Hugh's performance has been considerably better than Dermot and Hugh feels he has been carrying the business since the recession. Hugh's discontent peaked in July when all 20 sales of cares in the month were made by him. In August, Hugh's wife Annette formed a new company -- Annette's Wheels Limited -- to front a new car sales business for Hugh. Annette purchased stock from outlets that normally sold cars to the Company and always introduced herself as "Hugh's wife" making it clear that she was connected to Hugh and all purchases were made with the discounts negotiated by Hugh for the Company. Selling cars was the same -- Annette would contact people who had previously purchased cars from Hugh through the Company. In August and September the Company's business was badly affected and its sales were about 20% of what they had been. You are approached by Dermot for advice as to whether Hugh has been in breach of his duties as director of the Company and if he has, as to what remedies are open to Dermot and the Company. You are required to advise Dermot and the Company. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "A share is an intangible accumulation of rights, interests and obligations".
questions.text: "A share is an intangible accumulation of rights, interests and obligations". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Bob, Patrick and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company")… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Bob, Patrick and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company") which runs an estate agency. They are also shareholders in the Company and each hold 25 of the 100 issued shares, the other 25 shares being held by John. John is a solicitor and has never been a director of the company, being content to be just a shareholder. The premises from which the Company operates is owned by John and leased to the Company at a very favourable rent for the Company. Bob, Patrick and Patty have traditionally recommended that purchasers and vendors of properties that go through the agency use John's solicitor's practice for their conveyancing and this has been a very lucrative source of revenue to John. John would in turn recommend to his clients that they use the Company as their estate agent. In recent times, however, John's recommendations have been few and far between and Bob, Patrick and Patty have been recommending other firms of solicitors to clients of the Company as it seems to them that these other solicitors are in a better position than John to make cross-referrals back to the Company. On average, the Company would have made about 30 referrals to John every month; however, for the last 6 months, this has fallen steadily and last month there were only 5 referrals to him. John was unsure as to why referrals were falling off and had blamed it on the economic downturn until one of his friends in another firm of solicitors (who did not know John was a shareholder in the Company) mentioned that he and the Company were cross-referring clients very successfully. John was very annoyed and confronted Bob, Patrick and Patty, claiming that in ceasing to maintain his level of referrals they were acting in disregard of his interests as a shareholder. He also pointed to the below market rent he charged the Company for its premises. John also objected to not being told about their decision to make referrals elsewhere. Their response was that they were acting in the interests of the Company and had never asked for a lower rent and that he was welcome to seek to review the rent if he thought it was not at market value. They also indicated that if this was John's attitude, they would not make any referrals to him, a statement which provoked John to claim they were acting oppressively. You are required to advise John as to the following matters: (a) Whether John has a remedy under section 205 of the Companies Act 1963; and (b) Whether your answer would be different if the articles of association or a shareholders' agreement contained a provision that the Company would support shareholders' business by referring clients to them. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law. You must distinguish your answers to parts (a) and (b) of the question, both of which carry equal marks.

questions.description: Sarah and Andrew are directors of Famous Fillings Limited (the "Company")… Company, Director.
questions.text: Sarah and Andrew are directors of Famous Fillings Limited (the "Company") which is in the business of supplying sandwich fillings to coffee shops. The Company's business has held up reasonably well throughout the recession and it has been solvent. Some months ago one of the shops to which it supplies sandwich filling (Sean's Shop) complained that one of its customers who had bought a sandwich was suing the shop because the customer claimed he had found a mouse's tail in a tuna sandwich, which he had half eaten and which made him extremely ill. On receiving a solicitor's letter from the customer, Sean's Shop agreed to pay him €15,000 in compensation. This was accepted by the customer who had recently been made unemployed and Sean's Shop demanded that the Company reimburse the €15,000 compensation which it had paid. Sarah and Andrew have never before had such a claim and were shocked at the allegation that a mouse's tail could have found its way into their tuna mix. The area where the sandwich filling is prepared and packed is a sealed area and they are absolutely confident that the tail could not have found its way into the food before it left their premises, and so they have denied the claim and have refused to reimburse the shop. Sean's Shop wrote to the Company demanding that it pay the €15,000. When this was ignored Sean's Shop posted a demand and expressed to be pursuant to Section 214(a) of the Companies Act 1963 to the Company. Four weeks later the Company receives a petition from Sean's Shop to have the Company wound up on the grounds that it is unable to pay its debts and advising that the petition will be advertised in two newspapers in two days time. You are required to advise Sarah and Andrew. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "When a liquidator brings an application pursuant to section 150 of the Companies Act 1990 to have the director of an…
questions.text: "When a liquidator brings an application pursuant to section 150 of the Companies Act 1990 to have the director of an insolvent company restricted, the director's only hope of not being restricted is if he can show that he acted honestly and responsibly". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "Unlike meetings of members, the meetings of directors in a company are not heavily regulated by the Companies Acts".
questions.text: "Unlike meetings of members, the meetings of directors in a company are not heavily regulated by the Companies Acts". You are required to discuss this statement, stating whether you agree or disagree with it and must cite all relevant case and statute law in your answer.
